screens.subtitle: You just received a suspicious email. You've been told to only send money to vetted partners. Learn what to watch for and how to protect yourself and your organization.

screens.context: You are a new hire at EY who has just been onboarded onto the team. You receive an URGENT email from someone who’s claiming to be part of a financial team.

questions.question: What is the best course of action?

interactiveImage.imageAlt: NOT QUITE: Mock-up of a suspicious HR email in a workplace inbox

options.label: Ignore the message completely
options.description: Ignoring the message would still allow scammer to continuing disrupting others. Security teams want these types of emails reported. Select the correct answer to continue.

options.label: Click on the link to verify if this is a phishing scam or not
options.description: NOT QUITE: Interacting with the email in any way — even just clicking to check — puts you at risk. This can verify interaction and potentially trigger malware damages. Select the correct answer to continue.

options.label: Hover over the link to see the URL attached and alert management
options.description: CORRECT: Hovering allows you to inspect the link without click and alerting IT if you are suspicious would allow action to be taken. Click next to proceed

options.label: Send them an email back to start a conversation about the issue
options.description: NOT QUITE: Sending them an email back confirms to the scammer that your email is active. Select the correct answer to continue.

questions.question: What should you do after identifying the email as suspicious?

options.label: Report it through the proper channel
options.description: CORRECT: Reporting helps protect your organization / workplace and allows security teams to respond.

options.label: Leave it in your inbox and ignore it
options.description: NOT QUITE: This leaves the attacker roaming at large, even if the message doesn't impact you, it could impact others in the future. Select the correct answer to continue.

options.label: Forward the email to a colleague to show them
options.description: NOT QUITE: This spreads the threat of the phishing email. Unless told specifically by a proper channel, you shouldn't forward the email to anyone. Select the correct answer to continue.

options.label: Click on the links or attachments to confirm your suspicion
options.description: NOT QUITE: Clicking on links could lead your to 'spoofed' websites (websites designed to look normal but steal your credentials). Select the correct answer to continue.

questions.question: Who should this message be reported to?

options.label: The email client (via the 'report phishing' or 'report spam' button)
options.description: CORRECT: This will report the threat directly to the email service who will take action accordingly.

options.label: No one, just delete the email
options.description: NOT QUITE: This leaves the attacker roaming at large, even if the message doesn't impact you, it could impact others in the future. Select the correct answer to continue.

options.label: Reported back to the sender
options.description: NOT QUITE: This confirms that your email is active to the sender and you're someone willing to engage. Select the correct answer to continue.

options.label: Your company's IT or HR department.
options.description: CORRECT: Letting your company's security team know immediately will allow your company to log the threat and prevent the threat in the future.

screens.title: Common Patterns to Identify Phishing

interactiveImage.imageAlt: Clickable mock-up email with suspicious elements

screens.instructions: As you click through suspicious parts of the email, explanations will appear describing why each part is suspicious.

clickableElements.label: Suspicious sender domain
clickableElements.explanation: 'financialservices-secure.net' mimics legitimate domains — always verify the exact address.

clickableElements.label: Urgency & pressure tactics
clickableElements.explanation: '24 hours' deadlines create panic. Legitimate services rarely use such extreme urgency.

clickableElements.label: Suspicious link URL
clickableElements.explanation: The link domain doesn't match any known financial service. Hover before you click.

clickableElements.label: Unsolicited credential request
clickableElements.explanation: Real institutions don't ask for account details via email links.

screens.title: What should you do next time?
Module Complete
screens.reflection: Congrats on completing your interactive module!
takeaways: Actions you should take against potential phishing scams
